quick_facts.label: Conducting body
quick_facts.value: The National Testing Agency (NTA), an autonomous organization under India’s Ministry of Education, conducts CUET-UG.

quick_facts.label: What it is for
quick_facts.value: CUET-UG is a common entrance test whose scores are used by Central Universities and other participating universities/institutions for undergraduate admissions.

quick_facts.label: Eligibility baseline for CUET-UG 2026
quick_facts.value: There was no age limit; candidates who had passed Class 12/equivalent or were appearing in 2026 could sit the test, subject to the admission conditions of their target university and programme.

quick_facts.label: 2026 test structure
quick_facts.value: CUET-UG 2026 offered 37 tests—13 languages, 23 domain subjects, and one General Aptitude Test. A candidate could choose up to five tests.

quick_facts.label: 2026 delivery and marking
quick_facts.value: The 2026 examination used computer-based testing. Each test paper had 50 compulsory multiple-choice questions in 60 minutes, with +5 for a correct answer and −1 for an incorrect answer.

quick_facts.label: Score use
quick_facts.value: For the 2026 cycle, the NTA score was valid only for admission to the 2026–27 academic year; individual universities conducted their own merit-list and admission processes.

sections.heading: Purpose and place in undergraduate admissions
paragraphs: CUET-UG is a national common entrance examination for undergraduate programmes. NTA’s role is to register candidates, conduct the test, host answer-key challenges, declare NTA scores, and provide scorecards. The participating university—not NTA—sets the programme-level eligibility, prepares the merit list, and manages counselling or admission.

For students coming from arts, commerce, PCM, PCB, or PCMB in Classes 11–12, CUET-UG can support applications to a broad range of participating-university programmes. It is not itself an admission offer: a candidate must meet the specific university’s required Class 12 subjects, marks, category rules, documentation requirements, and any other admission conditions.
bullets: Use CUET-UG to seek admission to programmes at Central Universities and other participating state, deemed, private, autonomous, or other institutions.
Check the intended university’s programme mapping before selecting CUET papers; the participating-university list and programme offerings can change.
Do not assume that a CUET score alone guarantees a seat.

sections.heading: Eligibility: exam eligibility versus course eligibility
paragraphs: For CUET-UG 2026, NTA imposed no age limit. Candidates who had passed Class 12 (or an equivalent qualifying examination), or who were appearing in Class 12/equivalent in 2026, could take the examination. Recognized qualifying routes included 10+2 examinations of recognized boards, certain pre-university examinations, NIOS senior secondary examinations, specified diplomas, and recognized international qualifications.

This exam-level permission does not replace course eligibility. A university may impose its own age limit, minimum marks, required Class 12 subjects, accepted qualifying-examination year, reservation requirements, or additional criteria. Students should therefore first choose target programmes and then select only the CUET tests required by those programmes.
bullets: There was no CUET-UG 2026 age limit, but a target university could apply one.
Candidates could choose CUET tests irrespective of their Class 12 subjects for the exam; however, university programme requirements still governed admission eligibility.
Candidates with overseas or otherwise unlisted Class 12-equivalent qualifications may need an equivalence certificate as specified by NTA and the admitting university.

sections.heading: CUET-UG 2026 format
paragraphs: CUET-UG 2026 was conducted only as a computer-based test (CBT), in multiple shifts as required by candidate volume and subject choices. Question papers were available in 13 Indian languages: Assamese, Bengali, English, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Malayalam, Marathi, Odia, Punjabi, Tamil, Telugu, and Urdu. Where a bilingual paper was used, the English version prevailed in case of a discrepancy.

The 2026 catalogue comprised 13 language tests, 23 domain-specific tests, and the General Aptitude Test. Candidates could select a maximum of five tests, including languages and the General Aptitude Test. Each selected test paper had 50 compulsory objective MCQs and a 60-minute duration. The published marking rule was +5 for a correct response, −1 for an incorrect response, and 0 for an unanswered question.
bullets: Language tests assess reading comprehension, literary aptitude, and vocabulary.
Domain-subject syllabi are based on NCERT syllabi.
The General Aptitude Test covers general knowledge and current affairs, mental ability, numerical/quantitative reasoning, and logical and analytical reasoning.
PwBD candidates eligible for compensatory time were provided 20 minutes for each hour of examination.

sections.heading: Application process and 2026 fee context
paragraphs: The CUET-UG 2026 process was online through the NTA CUET portal. Applicants registered to obtain an application number, completed personal and education details, selected universities/programmes and test papers, chose examination-city preferences, uploaded the required photograph, signature, and applicable disability/identity documents, paid the fee online, and saved the confirmation page. NTA instructed candidates to submit only one application form.

For context only, the original 2026 bulletin scheduled applications for January 3–30, 2026, followed by a January 31 fee-payment deadline and a February 2–4 correction window; NTA subsequently issued separate notices about registration and deadline extensions. These dates are past and should not be reused for a later cycle. Prospective applicants should rely on the live CUET-UG notice board and the relevant year’s information bulletin.

The 2026 fee was variable by category and number of selected subjects. For up to three subjects, it was ₹1,000 for General candidates, ₹900 for OBC-NCL/EWS candidates, ₹800 for SC/ST/PwD/PwBD/third-gender candidates, and ₹4,500 at centres outside India; additional-subject charges applied. Bank/payment-gateway charges and GST could be additional. These are dated 2026 figures, not a forecast for future cycles.
bullets: Read the target university prospectus before submitting the form; confirm required CUET subjects, Class 12 prerequisites, minimum marks, and admission procedures.
Use an email address and mobile number that remain accessible, retain the application number, and download the confirmation page after successful payment.
Monitor official notices for any correction window, city-intimation slip, admit card, answer-key challenge, and scorecard updates.

sections.heading: Preparation approach
paragraphs: Begin with reverse planning from the intended degree and university. Identify each programme’s prescribed CUET subjects, then download the official syllabus for every selected language, domain subject, and/or General Aptitude Test. This matters especially for applicants whose school stream is broad: for example, a commerce, humanities, science, or vocational student should select papers based on the receiving programme’s stated requirements rather than on assumptions about the stream alone.

Prepare domain papers from the applicable NCERT-aligned syllabus, build language-test skill through timed comprehension, vocabulary, and literary-aptitude practice, and prepare General Aptitude through current-affairs awareness plus numerical, logical, and analytical-reasoning practice. Since every paper is a 60-minute CBT with all 50 questions compulsory and negative marking, use full-length computer-based practice to refine pacing, accuracy, and question-selection discipline.
bullets: Make a paper-selection sheet: target university, programme, mandatory Class 12 subjects, required CUET tests, and admission portal/counselling steps.
Use the official subject syllabus rather than relying solely on coaching-material topic lists.
Review mistakes by category—concept gap, careless error, time-management issue, or incorrect guess—to reduce the impact of −1 marking.
Keep university admissions separate from exam preparation: after results, monitor every target university’s merit-list and counselling notices.

sections.heading: Results, counselling, and next steps
paragraphs: NTA calculates results from the final answer key. For papers held in multiple shifts, raw marks are converted into an NTA Score using the published multi-shift procedure. In the 2026 cycle, NTA stated that the resulting score was valid only for the 2026–27 academic year, and that there would be no re-evaluation or re-checking after the result.

A CUET score enables participation in admissions at universities that accept the relevant score, but it does not grant admission automatically. Each institution applies its own programme eligibility, merit/rank rules, document verification, medical-fitness requirements where applicable, reservation rules, and counselling schedule. The student must therefore complete the separate university admission/counselling process and act on that university’s deadlines.

The range of possible academic outcomes includes undergraduate routes such as BA, B.Com, B.Sc, BBA, BCA, B.Voc, agriculture-related degrees, and integrated law or other programmes where a participating university lists CUET-UG for that course. Availability, required papers, seat counts, and admission cut-offs are university- and year-specific.
bullets: Download and retain the scorecard, confirmation page, and admit card.
Follow each target university’s admissions portal after NTA results; NTA does not conduct university counselling or publish the final institutional merit list.
Treat participating-university and programme lists as dynamic, and verify the current mapping directly with the university before making decisions.
